question: 3. Let \( a \) and \( b \) denote real numbers such that \( a<b \). The symbol \( (a, b) \) will denote the closed interval with the end points \( a, b \). Let there be given a collection of closed intervals \( \left(a_{1}, b_{1}\right), \ldots,\left(a_{n}, b_{n}\right) \) such that any two of these closed intervals have at least one point in common. Prove that there exists then a point which is contained in every one of these intervals.
solution:
Solution. Two closed intervals \( (c, d) \) and \( (e, f) \) overlap if and only it \( c \leq f \) and \( e \leq d \).

Since any two of the given intervals overlap we have
\[
a_{n} \leq b_{p}
\]
for all \( n \) and all \( p \). Hence \( \left\{a_{n}\right\} \) is bounded above (and non-empty) so we can let \( \xi \) be the least upper bound of \( \left\{a_{n}\right\} \). Since (1) shows that each \( b_{p} \) is an upper bound for \( \left\{a_{n}\right\} \), we have \( \xi \leq b_{p} \) for all \( p \), while \( a_{p} \leq \xi \) because \( \xi \) is an upper bound for \( \left\{a_{n}\right\} \). Thus \( \xi \in\left(a_{p}, b_{p}\right) \) for each \( p \).

Remarks. We have worded the proof so that it remains valid for an infinite collection of closed intervals that intersect in pairs. This is Helly's theorem in dimension one. See L. Danzer, B. Grunbaum, V. Klee, "Helly's Theorem and Its Relatives," Proc. Symposium in Pure Mathematics, 7: Convexity, American Mathematical Society, Providence, R.I., 1963. Also Yaglom and Boltyanskii, Convex Figures, Holt, Rinehart and Winston, New York, 1961.

descriptive_long.question: 3. Let \( leftend \) and \( rightend \) denote real numbers such that \( leftend<rightend \). The symbol \( (leftend, rightend) \) will denote the closed interval with the end points \( leftend, rightend \). Let there be given a collection of closed intervals \( \left(firstleft, firstright\right), \ldots,\left(indexleft, indexright\right) \) such that any two of these closed intervals have at least one point in common. Prove that there exists then a point which is contained in every one of these intervals.
descriptive_long.solution:
Solution. Two closed intervals \( (lefttemp, righttemp) \) and \( (secondleft, secondright) \) overlap if and only if \( lefttemp \leq secondright \) and \( secondleft \leq righttemp \).

Since any two of the given intervals overlap we have
\[
indexleft \leq prightend
\]
for all \( n \) and all \( p \). Hence \( \left\{indexleft\right\} \) is bounded above (and non-empty) so we can let \( supremum \) be the least upper bound of \( \left\{indexleft\right\} \). Since (1) shows that each \( prightend \) is an upper bound for \( \left\{indexleft\right\} \), we have \( supremum \leq prightend \) for all \( p \), while \( pleftend \leq supremum \) because \( supremum \) is an upper bound for \( \left\{indexleft\right\} \). Thus \( supremum \in\left(pleftend, prightend\right) \) for each \( p \).

Remarks. We have worded the proof so that it remains valid for an infinite collection of closed intervals that intersect in pairs. This is Helly's theorem in dimension one. See L. Danzer, B. Grunbaum, V. Klee, \"Helly's Theorem and Its Relatives,\" Proc. Symposium in Pure Mathematics, 7: Convexity, American Mathematical Society, Providence, R.I., 1963. Also Yaglom and Boltyanskii, Convex Figures, Holt, Rinehart and Winston, New York, 1961.

descriptive_long_confusing.question: 3. Let \( sandpaper \) and \( wheelhouse \) denote real numbers such that \( sandpaper<wheelhouse \). The symbol \( (sandpaper, wheelhouse) \) will denote the closed interval with the end points \( sandpaper, wheelhouse \). Let there be given a collection of closed intervals \( \left(blueberry, raincloud\right), \ldots,\left(doorstep, hummingbird\right) \) such that any two of these closed intervals have at least one point in common. Prove that there exists then a point which is contained in every one of these intervals.
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
Solution. Two closed intervals \( (moonlight, printerink) \) and \( (sunflower, babysteps) \) overlap if and only it \( moonlight \leq babysteps \) and \( sunflower \leq printerink \).

Since any two of the given intervals overlap we have
\[
doorstep \leq paperclips
\]
for all \( n \) and all \( p \). Hence \( \left\{doorstep\right\} \) is bounded above (and non-empty) so we can let \( cottonseed \) be the least upper bound of \( \left\{doorstep\right\} \). Since (1) shows that each \( paperclips \) is an upper bound for \( \left\{doorstep\right\} \), we have \( cottonseed \leq paperclips \) for all \( p \), while \( skylights \leq cottonseed \) because \( cottonseed \) is an upper bound for \( \left\{doorstep\right\} \). Thus \( cottonseed \in\left(skylights, paperclips\right) \) for each \( p \).

Remarks. We have worded the proof so that it remains valid for an infinite collection of closed intervals that intersect in pairs. This is Helly's theorem in dimension one. See L. Danzer, B. Grunbaum, V. Klee, "Helly's Theorem and Its Relatives," Proc. Symposium in Pure Mathematics, 7: Convexity, American Mathematical Society, Providence, R.I., 1963. Also Yaglom and Boltyanskii, Convex Figures, Holt, Rinehart and Winston, New York, 1961.

descriptive_long_misleading.question: 3. Let \( uppermargin \) and \( lowermargin \) denote real numbers such that \( uppermargin<lowermargin \). The symbol \( (uppermargin, lowermargin) \) will denote the closed interval with the end points \( uppermargin, lowermargin \). Let there be given a collection of closed intervals \( \left(upperfirst, lowerfirst\right), \ldots,\left(uppergeneral, lowergeneral\right) \) such that any two of these closed intervals have at least one point in common. Prove that there exists then a point which is contained in every one of these intervals.
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
Solution. Two closed intervals \( (higherlimit, lowerlimit) \) and \( (topboundary, bottombound) \) overlap if and only if \( higherlimit \leq bottombound \) and \( topboundary \leq lowerlimit \).

Since any two of the given intervals overlap we have
\[
uppergeneral \leq lowerparticular
\]
for all \( n \) and all \( p \). Hence \( \left\{uppergeneral\right\} \) is bounded above (and non-empty) so we can let \( inferior \) be the least upper bound of \( \left\{uppergeneral\right\} \). Since (1) shows that each \( lowerparticular \) is an upper bound for \( \left\{uppergeneral\right\} \), we have \( inferior \leq lowerparticular \) for all \( p \), while \( upperparticular \leq inferior \) because \( inferior \) is an upper bound for \( \left\{uppergeneral\right\} \). Thus \( inferior \in\left(upperparticular, lowerparticular\right) \) for each \( p \).

Remarks. We have worded the proof so that it remains valid for an infinite collection of closed intervals that intersect in pairs. This is Helly's theorem in dimension one. See L. Danzer, B. Grunbaum, V. Klee, "Helly's Theorem and Its Relatives," Proc. Symposium in Pure Mathematics, 7: Convexity, American Mathematical Society, Providence, R.I., 1963. Also Yaglom and Boltyanskii, Convex Figures, Holt, Rinehart and Winston, New York, 1961.

garbled_string.question: 3. Let \( kjsnqpfm \) and \( zcnmwtrh \) denote real numbers such that \( kjsnqpfm<zcnmwtrh \). The symbol \( (kjsnqpfm, zcnmwtrh) \) will denote the closed interval with the end points \( kjsnqpfm, zcnmwtrh \). Let there be given a collection of closed intervals \( \left(pqvmksal, ldfgrnce\right), \ldots,\left(cpfshjra, xzlhgnop\right) \) such that any two of these closed intervals have at least one point in common. Prove that there exists then a point which is contained in every one of these intervals.
garbled_string.solution:
Solution. Two closed intervals \( (nbztrflk, hmjgrsca) \) and \( (qprnvldk, sntlwgav) \) overlap if and only it \( nbztrflk \leq sntlwgav \) and \( qprnvldk \leq hmjgrsca \).

Since any two of the given intervals overlap we have
\[
cpfshjra \leq vlsgcnat
\]
for all \( n \) and all \( p \). Hence \( \left\{cpfshjra\right\} \) is bounded above (and non-empty) so we can let \( jdflqwer \) be the least upper bound of \( \left\{cpfshjra\right\} \). Since (1) shows that each \( vlsgcnat \) is an upper bound for \( \left\{cpfshjra\right\} \), we have \( jdflqwer \leq vlsgcnat \) for all \( p \), while \( brqltwex \leq jdflqwer \) because \( jdflqwer \) is an upper bound for \( \left\{cpfshjra\right\} \). Thus \( jdflqwer \in\left(brqltwex, vlsgcnat\right) \) for each \( p \).

Remarks. We have worded the proof so that it remains valid for an infinite collection of closed intervals that intersect in pairs. This is Helly's theorem in dimension one. See L. Danzer, B. Grunbaum, V. Klee, "Helly's Theorem and Its Relatives," Proc. Symposium in Pure Mathematics, 7: Convexity, American Mathematical Society, Providence, R.I., 1963. Also Yaglom and Boltyanskii, Convex Figures, Holt, Rinehart and Winston, New York, 1961.

kernel_variant.question:
(Sharp Quantitative Helly-type Theorem for Half-spaces)

Fix an integer $d\ge 1$ and a real number $R>0$.  
Let $A$ be an (arbitrary, possibly infinite) index set with cardinality  
\[
|A|\;\ge\;d+1 .
\tag{C}
\]  
For every $\alpha\in A$ put  
\[
H_{\alpha}\;=\;\bigl\{x\in\mathbb{R}^{d}\;:\;\langle v_{\alpha},x\rangle\le b_{\alpha}\bigr\},
\qquad v_{\alpha}\in\mathbb{R}^{d}\setminus\{0\},\; b_{\alpha}\in\mathbb{R},
\]
a closed affine half-space.

Assume the following \emph{quantitative $(d+1)$-wise intersection property}:

(Q)  
(i)  For every finite subfamily $\{\alpha_{1},\dots ,\alpha_{k}\}\subset A$ with $k\le d+1$ one has  
\[
H_{\alpha_{1}}\cap\dots\cap H_{\alpha_{k}}\neq\varnothing;
\tag{Q1}
\]

(ii)  Whenever $k=d+1$ the whole intersection already lies in the Euclidean ball  
\[
H_{\alpha_{1}}\cap\dots\cap H_{\alpha_{d+1}}\;\subset\;B(0,R),
\qquad 
B(0,R):=\bigl\{x\in\mathbb{R}^{d}: \|x\|_{2}\le R\bigr\}.
\tag{Q2}
\]

Prove that

1.\; the total intersection $\displaystyle\bigcap_{\alpha\in A}H_{\alpha}$ is non-empty;

2.\; every point of this intersection in fact lies in $B(0,R)$, i.e.  
   $\displaystyle\bigcap_{\alpha\in A}H_{\alpha}\;\subset\;B(0,R)$;

3.\; the number $d+1$ occurring in (Q) is best possible:  
   for every integer $d\ge 1$ and every radius $R>0$ there exists a family
   $\{H_{\alpha}\}_{\alpha\in A}$ of closed half-spaces in $\mathbb{R}^{d}$ satisfying

   (a) property (Q1) for \emph{every} subfamily of at most $d$ members,  

   but such that

   (b) $\displaystyle\bigcap_{\alpha\in A}H_{\alpha}=\varnothing$.

   Consequently the quantitative conclusion in 2.\ cannot, in general,
   be guaranteed if one only knows the validity of (Q1) for subfamilies of
   size $\le d$.

-------------------------------------------------------------
kernel_variant.solution:
We treat the three assertions in turn.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
1.\; Non-emptiness of the global intersection.

Introduce the auxiliary convex sets  
\[
K_{\alpha}:=H_{\alpha}\cap B(0,R)\qquad (\alpha\in A).
\]
Because $B(0,R)$ is compact and convex, each $K_{\alpha}$ is a closed convex
subset of a compact set; hence every finite intersection of the $K_{\alpha}$
is compact.

\medskip
\emph{Step 1.1 - Finite subfamilies of size $\le d+1$.}  
Let $\{\alpha_{1},\dots ,\alpha_{k}\}\subset A$ with $k\le d+1$.

$\bullet$ If $k=d+1$, then (Q2) gives
\[
K_{\alpha_{1}}\cap\dots\cap K_{\alpha_{d+1}}
=\bigl(H_{\alpha_{1}}\cap\dots\cap H_{\alpha_{d+1}}\bigr)\cap B(0,R)
=H_{\alpha_{1}}\cap\dots\cap H_{\alpha_{d+1}}\neq\varnothing .
\]

$\bullet$ Suppose $k<d+1$.  Because of the cardinality condition (C) we can
choose additional, pairwise distinct indices
$\alpha_{k+1},\dots ,\alpha_{d+1}\in A\setminus\{\alpha_{1},\dots ,\alpha_{k}\}$.
Applying (Q2) to the enlarged $(d+1)$-tuple we get a point
\[
x^{\ast}\in H_{\alpha_{1}}\cap\dots\cap H_{\alpha_{d+1}}\subset B(0,R),
\]
hence $x^{\ast}\in K_{\alpha_{j}}$ for every $1\le j\le k$.  Therefore  
\[
K_{\alpha_{1}}\cap\dots\cap K_{\alpha_{k}}\neq\varnothing .
\tag{1.1}
\]

\medskip
\emph{Step 1.2 - Arbitrary finite subfamilies.}  
Now let $J\subset A$ be \emph{any} finite index set.  
Because of (1.1), every subfamily of
$\{K_{\alpha}\}_{\alpha\in J}$ containing at most $d+1$ members has
non-empty intersection.  Helly's theorem for \emph{finite} families of convex
sets in $\mathbb{R}^{d}$ therefore implies  
\[
\bigcap_{\alpha\in J}K_{\alpha}\neq\varnothing.
\tag{1.2}
\]

\medskip
\emph{Step 1.3 - Passing to the whole family.}  
Relation (1.2) shows that the collection $\{K_{\alpha}\}_{\alpha\in A}$
has the \emph{finite intersection property}.  
Since every $K_{\alpha}$ is a closed subset of the compact set $B(0,R)$,
compactness yields  
\[
\bigcap_{\alpha\in A}K_{\alpha}\neq\varnothing .
\]
Choose $x^{\ast}$ in that intersection.  Then $x^{\ast}\in H_{\alpha}$ for
every $\alpha$ and $x^{\ast}\in B(0,R)$, proving  
\[
\bigcap_{\alpha\in A}H_{\alpha}\neq\varnothing .
\]

--------------------------------------------------------------------
2.\; A uniform radius bound for the whole intersection.

Let $x\in\bigcap_{\alpha\in A}H_{\alpha}$ and suppose,
for a contradiction, that $\|x\|_{2}>R$.  
Because $|A|\ge d+1$, we can pick distinct indices
$\alpha_{1},\dots ,\alpha_{d+1}\in A$.  
Then $x$ lies in the intersection
$H_{\alpha_{1}}\cap\dots\cap H_{\alpha_{d+1}}$,
yet by (Q2) this set is contained in $B(0,R)$,
contradicting $\|x\|_{2}>R$.  Therefore
$\|x\|_{2}\le R$ for every $x$ in the total intersection, and we have  
\[
\bigcap_{\alpha\in A}H_{\alpha}\;\subset\;B(0,R).
\]

--------------------------------------------------------------------
3.\; Optimality of the number $d+1$.

We show that the quantitative Helly statement fails
if one is only allowed to inspect subfamilies of \emph{at most $d$} members.

\smallskip
\textbf{Step 3.1:  Any non-empty intersection of at most $d$ half-spaces is unbounded.}

Let $1\le k\le d$ and let $G_{1},\dots ,G_{k}$ be closed half-spaces in
$\mathbb{R}^{d}$ with non-empty intersection
$G:=\bigcap_{i=1}^{k}G_{i}$.  
Write $G_{i}=\{x\in\mathbb{R}^{d}:\langle a^{i},x\rangle\le b_{i}\}$ with
$a^{i}\neq 0$ for $1\le i\le k$, and set  
\[
A:=\begin{pmatrix}(a^{1})^{\top}\\\vdots\\(a^{k})^{\top}\end{pmatrix}\in\mathbb{R}^{k\times d}.
\]
Choose $x_{0}\in G$.

\emph{Case 1: $\operatorname{rank}A\le k-1$.}  
Then $\dim\ker A\ge 1$; pick a non-zero $w\in\ker A$.  
For every $t\ge 0$,
$\langle a^{i},x_{0}+tw\rangle=\langle a^{i},x_{0}\rangle\le b_{i}$,
so $x_{0}+tw\in G$.  Hence $G$ contains the unbounded ray
$\{x_{0}+tw:t\ge 0\}$.

\emph{Case 2: $\operatorname{rank}A=k=d$.}  
Then $A$ is invertible.  Put
$w:=-A^{-1}\mathbf 1$, $\mathbf 1:=(1,\dots ,1)^{\top}$.  
Because $Aw=-\mathbf 1$, one has $\langle a^{i},w\rangle=-1$ for all $i$.
For $t>0$,
$\langle a^{i},x_{0}+tw\rangle=\langle a^{i},x_{0}\rangle-t\le b_{i}-t<b_{i}$,
so $x_{0}+tw\in G$ for every $t>0$.  
Again $G$ contains the entire ray $\{x_{0}+tw:t\ge 0\}$.

\emph{Case 3: $k<d$ and $\operatorname{rank}A=k$.}  
Here $d-k\ge 1$, so $\ker A$ is non-trivial; choose $0\neq w\in\ker A$
and repeat the argument of Case~1.  

\noindent
Combining the three cases we have established  
\[
\text{every non-empty intersection of at most $d$ half-spaces in $\mathbb{R}^{d}$ is unbounded.}
\tag{3.1}
\]

\smallskip
\textbf{Step 3.2:  A concrete counterexample for the $d$-wise hypothesis.}

Fix $R>0$ and set $c:=R+1$.  
Define the following $d+1$ closed half-spaces in $\mathbb{R}^{d}$:
\[
\begin{aligned}
H_{0}&:=\Bigl\{x=(x_{1},\dots ,x_{d})\in\mathbb{R}^{d}\;:\;
           \sum_{i=1}^{d}x_{i}\le 0\Bigr\},\\[4pt]
H_{i}&:=\Bigl\{x=(x_{1},\dots ,x_{d})\in\mathbb{R}^{d}\;:\;
           x_{i}\ge c\Bigr\}, \qquad 1\le i\le d.
\end{aligned}
\tag{3.2}
\]

(a) \emph{Every subfamily of at most $d$ members has non-empty intersection.}

$\bullet$ If the subfamily omits $H_{0}$, it is of the form
$\{H_{i}\}_{i\in I}$ with $I\subseteq\{1,\dots ,d\}$ and $|I|\le d$.
The point $x^{(I)}$ defined by  
\[
x^{(I)}_{i}=c \text{ for } i\in I,\qquad 
x^{(I)}_{i}=c+1 \text{ for } i\notin I
\]
lies in all selected half-spaces, so the intersection is non-empty (and unbounded).

$\bullet$ If the subfamily contains $H_{0}$ but omits, say, $H_{k}$ with $k\ge 1$, take  
\[
x_{k}:=-d(c+1),\qquad
x_{i}=c\quad(i\neq k).
\]
Then $\sum_{i=1}^{d}x_{i}= -d(c+1)+(d-1)c=-c<0$, so the point
$x=(x_{1},\dots ,x_{d})$ satisfies $H_{0}$ as well as every
$H_{i}$ with $i\neq k$.  Hence the intersection is again non-empty (and
unbounded).

Thus property (Q1) holds for \emph{every} subfamily of at most $d$ members.

(b) \emph{The total intersection is empty.}

If $x\in\bigcap_{i=0}^{d}H_{i}$ we would have
$x_{i}\ge c$ for all $1\le i\le d$, hence
$\sum_{i=1}^{d}x_{i}\ge d\,c>0$, contradicting the defining inequality of
$H_{0}$.  Consequently  
\[
\bigcap_{i=0}^{d}H_{i}=\varnothing .
\tag{3.3}
\]

\smallskip
\textbf{Step 3.3:  Consequences.}

The family $\{H_{0},\dots ,H_{d}\}$ satisfies (a) and (b) above, establishing the optimality of the number $d+1$.  
Moreover, by (3.1) every $d$-wise intersection is unbounded, so
\emph{no} ball $B(0,R)$ can cover all those intersections.  Therefore the radius
bound asserted in part~2 cannot be guaranteed under a merely
$d$-wise hypothesis, and the parameter $d+1$ is optimal.

\hfill$\square$

---------------------------------------------------------------------

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
• Higher dimension:  the problem is set in ℝᵈ for arbitrary d, not in ℝ.  
• Additional constraints:  the “radius-R” quantitative restriction forces the solver
  to keep track of the size of every finite intersection, not merely its existence.  
• Sophisticated structure:  half–spaces are unbounded; lifting to ℝ^{d+1} produces
  cones and cylinders, so the argument mixes affine geometry with norm constraints.  
• Deeper theory:  a successful solution needs the classical Helly theorem, but also
  must recognise how to convert a norm bound into a linear inequality in one higher
  dimension – a standard trick in convex analysis but far from obvious at
  Olympiad level.  
• Interacting concepts:  convexity, compactness of finite intersections,
  quantitative vs. qualitative Helly, and a non-trivial minimal-example argument
  all appear.

These layers of technicality and the necessity of invoking higher-dimensional
Helly put the problem well beyond the scope of the original one-dimensional
interval exercise.

original_kernel_variant.question:
(Sharp Quantitative Helly-type Theorem for Half-spaces)

Fix an integer $d\ge 1$ and a real number $R>0$.  
Let $A$ be an (arbitrary, possibly infinite) index set with cardinality  
\[
|A|\;\ge\;d+1 .
\tag{C}
\]  
For every $\alpha\in A$ put  
\[
H_{\alpha}\;=\;\bigl\{x\in\mathbb{R}^{d}\;:\;\langle v_{\alpha},x\rangle\le b_{\alpha}\bigr\},
\qquad v_{\alpha}\in\mathbb{R}^{d}\setminus\{0\},\; b_{\alpha}\in\mathbb{R},
\]
a closed affine half-space.

Assume the following \emph{quantitative $(d+1)$-wise intersection property}:

(Q)  
(i)  For every finite subfamily $\{\alpha_{1},\dots ,\alpha_{k}\}\subset A$ with $k\le d+1$ one has  
\[
H_{\alpha_{1}}\cap\dots\cap H_{\alpha_{k}}\neq\varnothing;
\tag{Q1}
\]

(ii)  Whenever $k=d+1$ the whole intersection already lies in the Euclidean ball  
\[
H_{\alpha_{1}}\cap\dots\cap H_{\alpha_{d+1}}\;\subset\;B(0,R),
\qquad 
B(0,R):=\bigl\{x\in\mathbb{R}^{d}: \|x\|_{2}\le R\bigr\}.
\tag{Q2}
\]

Prove that

1.\; the total intersection $\displaystyle\bigcap_{\alpha\in A}H_{\alpha}$ is non-empty;

2.\; every point of this intersection in fact lies in $B(0,R)$, i.e.  
   $\displaystyle\bigcap_{\alpha\in A}H_{\alpha}\;\subset\;B(0,R)$;

3.\; the number $d+1$ occurring in (Q) is best possible:  
   for every integer $d\ge 1$ and every radius $R>0$ there exists a family
   $\{H_{\alpha}\}_{\alpha\in A}$ of closed half-spaces in $\mathbb{R}^{d}$ satisfying

   (a) property (Q1) for \emph{every} subfamily of at most $d$ members,  

   but such that

   (b) $\displaystyle\bigcap_{\alpha\in A}H_{\alpha}=\varnothing$.

   Consequently the quantitative conclusion in 2.\ cannot, in general,
   be guaranteed if one only knows the validity of (Q1) for subfamilies of
   size $\le d$.

-------------------------------------------------------------
original_kernel_variant.solution:
We treat the three assertions in turn.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
1.\; Non-emptiness of the global intersection.

Introduce the auxiliary convex sets  
\[
K_{\alpha}:=H_{\alpha}\cap B(0,R)\qquad (\alpha\in A).
\]
Because $B(0,R)$ is compact and convex, each $K_{\alpha}$ is a closed convex
subset of a compact set; hence every finite intersection of the $K_{\alpha}$
is compact.

\medskip
\emph{Step 1.1 - Finite subfamilies of size $\le d+1$.}  
Let $\{\alpha_{1},\dots ,\alpha_{k}\}\subset A$ with $k\le d+1$.

$\bullet$ If $k=d+1$, then (Q2) gives
\[
K_{\alpha_{1}}\cap\dots\cap K_{\alpha_{d+1}}
=\bigl(H_{\alpha_{1}}\cap\dots\cap H_{\alpha_{d+1}}\bigr)\cap B(0,R)
=H_{\alpha_{1}}\cap\dots\cap H_{\alpha_{d+1}}\neq\varnothing .
\]

$\bullet$ Suppose $k<d+1$.  Because of the cardinality condition (C) we can
choose additional, pairwise distinct indices
$\alpha_{k+1},\dots ,\alpha_{d+1}\in A\setminus\{\alpha_{1},\dots ,\alpha_{k}\}$.
Applying (Q2) to the enlarged $(d+1)$-tuple we get a point
\[
x^{\ast}\in H_{\alpha_{1}}\cap\dots\cap H_{\alpha_{d+1}}\subset B(0,R),
\]
hence $x^{\ast}\in K_{\alpha_{j}}$ for every $1\le j\le k$.  Therefore  
\[
K_{\alpha_{1}}\cap\dots\cap K_{\alpha_{k}}\neq\varnothing .
\tag{1.1}
\]

\medskip
\emph{Step 1.2 - Arbitrary finite subfamilies.}  
Now let $J\subset A$ be \emph{any} finite index set.  
Because of (1.1), every subfamily of
$\{K_{\alpha}\}_{\alpha\in J}$ containing at most $d+1$ members has
non-empty intersection.  Helly's theorem for \emph{finite} families of convex
sets in $\mathbb{R}^{d}$ therefore implies  
\[
\bigcap_{\alpha\in J}K_{\alpha}\neq\varnothing.
\tag{1.2}
\]

\medskip
\emph{Step 1.3 - Passing to the whole family.}  
Relation (1.2) shows that the collection $\{K_{\alpha}\}_{\alpha\in A}$
has the \emph{finite intersection property}.  
Since every $K_{\alpha}$ is a closed subset of the compact set $B(0,R)$,
compactness yields  
\[
\bigcap_{\alpha\in A}K_{\alpha}\neq\varnothing .
\]
Choose $x^{\ast}$ in that intersection.  Then $x^{\ast}\in H_{\alpha}$ for
every $\alpha$ and $x^{\ast}\in B(0,R)$, proving  
\[
\bigcap_{\alpha\in A}H_{\alpha}\neq\varnothing .
\]

--------------------------------------------------------------------
2.\; A uniform radius bound for the whole intersection.

Let $x\in\bigcap_{\alpha\in A}H_{\alpha}$ and suppose,
for a contradiction, that $\|x\|_{2}>R$.  
Because $|A|\ge d+1$, we can pick distinct indices
$\alpha_{1},\dots ,\alpha_{d+1}\in A$.  
Then $x$ lies in the intersection
$H_{\alpha_{1}}\cap\dots\cap H_{\alpha_{d+1}}$,
yet by (Q2) this set is contained in $B(0,R)$,
contradicting $\|x\|_{2}>R$.  Therefore
$\|x\|_{2}\le R$ for every $x$ in the total intersection, and we have  
\[
\bigcap_{\alpha\in A}H_{\alpha}\;\subset\;B(0,R).
\]

--------------------------------------------------------------------
3.\; Optimality of the number $d+1$.

We show that the quantitative Helly statement fails
if one is only allowed to inspect subfamilies of \emph{at most $d$} members.

\smallskip
\textbf{Step 3.1:  Any non-empty intersection of at most $d$ half-spaces is unbounded.}

Let $1\le k\le d$ and let $G_{1},\dots ,G_{k}$ be closed half-spaces in
$\mathbb{R}^{d}$ with non-empty intersection
$G:=\bigcap_{i=1}^{k}G_{i}$.  
Write $G_{i}=\{x\in\mathbb{R}^{d}:\langle a^{i},x\rangle\le b_{i}\}$ with
$a^{i}\neq 0$ for $1\le i\le k$, and set  
\[
A:=\begin{pmatrix}(a^{1})^{\top}\\\vdots\\(a^{k})^{\top}\end{pmatrix}\in\mathbb{R}^{k\times d}.
\]
Choose $x_{0}\in G$.

\emph{Case 1: $\operatorname{rank}A\le k-1$.}  
Then $\dim\ker A\ge 1$; pick a non-zero $w\in\ker A$.  
For every $t\ge 0$,
$\langle a^{i},x_{0}+tw\rangle=\langle a^{i},x_{0}\rangle\le b_{i}$,
so $x_{0}+tw\in G$.  Hence $G$ contains the unbounded ray
$\{x_{0}+tw:t\ge 0\}$.

\emph{Case 2: $\operatorname{rank}A=k=d$.}  
Then $A$ is invertible.  Put
$w:=-A^{-1}\mathbf 1$, $\mathbf 1:=(1,\dots ,1)^{\top}$.  
Because $Aw=-\mathbf 1$, one has $\langle a^{i},w\rangle=-1$ for all $i$.
For $t>0$,
$\langle a^{i},x_{0}+tw\rangle=\langle a^{i},x_{0}\rangle-t\le b_{i}-t<b_{i}$,
so $x_{0}+tw\in G$ for every $t>0$.  
Again $G$ contains the entire ray $\{x_{0}+tw:t\ge 0\}$.

\emph{Case 3: $k<d$ and $\operatorname{rank}A=k$.}  
Here $d-k\ge 1$, so $\ker A$ is non-trivial; choose $0\neq w\in\ker A$
and repeat the argument of Case~1.  

\noindent
Combining the three cases we have established  
\[
\text{every non-empty intersection of at most $d$ half-spaces in $\mathbb{R}^{d}$ is unbounded.}
\tag{3.1}
\]

\smallskip
\textbf{Step 3.2:  A concrete counterexample for the $d$-wise hypothesis.}

Fix $R>0$ and set $c:=R+1$.  
Define the following $d+1$ closed half-spaces in $\mathbb{R}^{d}$:
\[
\begin{aligned}
H_{0}&:=\Bigl\{x=(x_{1},\dots ,x_{d})\in\mathbb{R}^{d}\;:\;
           \sum_{i=1}^{d}x_{i}\le 0\Bigr\},\\[4pt]
H_{i}&:=\Bigl\{x=(x_{1},\dots ,x_{d})\in\mathbb{R}^{d}\;:\;
           x_{i}\ge c\Bigr\}, \qquad 1\le i\le d.
\end{aligned}
\tag{3.2}
\]

(a) \emph{Every subfamily of at most $d$ members has non-empty intersection.}

$\bullet$ If the subfamily omits $H_{0}$, it is of the form
$\{H_{i}\}_{i\in I}$ with $I\subseteq\{1,\dots ,d\}$ and $|I|\le d$.
The point $x^{(I)}$ defined by  
\[
x^{(I)}_{i}=c \text{ for } i\in I,\qquad 
x^{(I)}_{i}=c+1 \text{ for } i\notin I
\]
lies in all selected half-spaces, so the intersection is non-empty (and unbounded).

$\bullet$ If the subfamily contains $H_{0}$ but omits, say, $H_{k}$ with $k\ge 1$, take  
\[
x_{k}:=-d(c+1),\qquad
x_{i}=c\quad(i\neq k).
\]
Then $\sum_{i=1}^{d}x_{i}= -d(c+1)+(d-1)c=-c<0$, so the point
$x=(x_{1},\dots ,x_{d})$ satisfies $H_{0}$ as well as every
$H_{i}$ with $i\neq k$.  Hence the intersection is again non-empty (and
unbounded).

Thus property (Q1) holds for \emph{every} subfamily of at most $d$ members.

(b) \emph{The total intersection is empty.}

If $x\in\bigcap_{i=0}^{d}H_{i}$ we would have
$x_{i}\ge c$ for all $1\le i\le d$, hence
$\sum_{i=1}^{d}x_{i}\ge d\,c>0$, contradicting the defining inequality of
$H_{0}$.  Consequently  
\[
\bigcap_{i=0}^{d}H_{i}=\varnothing .
\tag{3.3}
\]

\smallskip
\textbf{Step 3.3:  Consequences.}

The family $\{H_{0},\dots ,H_{d}\}$ satisfies (a) and (b) above, establishing the optimality of the number $d+1$.  
Moreover, by (3.1) every $d$-wise intersection is unbounded, so
\emph{no} ball $B(0,R)$ can cover all those intersections.  Therefore the radius
bound asserted in part~2 cannot be guaranteed under a merely
$d$-wise hypothesis, and the parameter $d+1$ is optimal.

\hfill$\square$

---------------------------------------------------------------------

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
• Higher dimension:  the problem is set in ℝᵈ for arbitrary d, not in ℝ.  
• Additional constraints:  the “radius-R” quantitative restriction forces the solver
  to keep track of the size of every finite intersection, not merely its existence.  
• Sophisticated structure:  half–spaces are unbounded; lifting to ℝ^{d+1} produces
  cones and cylinders, so the argument mixes affine geometry with norm constraints.  
• Deeper theory:  a successful solution needs the classical Helly theorem, but also
  must recognise how to convert a norm bound into a linear inequality in one higher
  dimension – a standard trick in convex analysis but far from obvious at
  Olympiad level.  
• Interacting concepts:  convexity, compactness of finite intersections,
  quantitative vs. qualitative Helly, and a non-trivial minimal-example argument
  all appear.

These layers of technicality and the necessity of invoking higher-dimensional
Helly put the problem well beyond the scope of the original one-dimensional
interval exercise.
